subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: Addition and subtraction are the chapter's organising purpose throughout. Children encounter three distinct strategies for 15+7 on the ginladi (counting on by ones, making the nearest ten then stepping on, overshooting and jumping back), then extend this to sums and differences up to and beyond 100 via the stamp-collection story, radish-field harvest, tomato-carrot box, and apple-plucking contexts. The five structured word problems on the final pages require children to choose a representation (box diagram, matchstick bundles, or number line) and apply the correct operation — directly exercising the competency's call to apply the four basic operations to solve daily-life problems.

A substantive pattern-recognition strand runs through the chapter independently of the computation work. The frog jumping forward by 10 from 7, then from 23, and the grasshopper jumping backward by 10 from 76 establish skip-counting sequences that children must mark and extend on the ginladi. The three jump-table tasks (filling sequences with fixed steps of 5, 6, and 9 from assorted starting points) further require children to identify and continue additive patterns. The Magic Sums section adds a structural pattern layer — children observe that rows, columns, and diagonals of the 3×3 grid all sum to the same value.

adjunct_competencies.justification: The Magic Sums section is an explicit recreational mathematics puzzle: children complete partially-filled number grids (using digits 1–9, each once) so that every row and column sums to a given target, and then complete a full magic square given only three entries. The Card Game (finding three cards from 1–10 that form an addition or subtraction statement) and the Race-to-100 two-player game are further puzzle contexts where number relationships are the object of play.

The chapter consistently foregrounds method selection rather than a single prescribed algorithm. Three characters model three different ginladi strategies for the same computation; the word-problem section explicitly instructs children to first draw a box diagram and then choose between matchstick bundles and the number line; and the estimation-and-match task on the final page requires children to reason about the size of an answer before computing. These are all instances of selecting an appropriate method or tool in accordance with context.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to add and subtract whole numbers fluently by working across multiple concrete models — bead string, tens frame, matchstick bundles, and 100-number grid — while also recognising and extending additive number patterns through skip-jumping and jump-table tasks.
